title: Beyond Monolithic Scaling: Modularity and Heterogeneity as an Architectural Imperative for Utility-Scale Quantum Computing
authors: Bo Fan, Renzhou Fang, Yuntao Zhang, Xiaolong Yuan, Dafa Zhao
abstract: Scalable quantum computing is fundamentally bottlenecked not by qubit count or fabrication yield, but by a rigid temporal mismatch: macroscopic classical coordination latency ($τ_c$) inevitably grows with system diameter, while microscopic quantum coherence ($τ_q$) remains strictly bounded. Beyond a critical scale, this mismatch breaches the classical control light cone, triggering a superlinear geometric penalty ($ε> 0$) that renders monolithic synchronization physically impossible. We formalize the resulting structural phase transition through a governing scaling law, $1+ε> γ$, which mandates modular decomposition and a shift from global unitaries to Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC). To manage the resulting resource contention under strict coherence budgets, we introduce a layered semantic architecture and a time-aware Reserve--Commit protocol. By embedding predictive temporal pre-validation, the protocol acts as an architectural semantic classifier: it preemptively aborts transactions that exceed the causal horizon and explicitly converts scheduling-induced failures into location-known erasure metadata, directly relaxing hardware fidelity thresholds for downstream QEC decoders. Under near-term transduction targets ($η_{\mathrm{trans}} \sim 0.1$), we project a crossover scale at $N_c \sim 10^5$--$10^6$ physical qubits. This threshold marks a profound architectural convergence: the footprint required for modularity aligns precisely with early fault-tolerant utility, establishing time-aware distributed orchestration, rather than monolithic expansion or centralized classical control, as the physical imperative for utility-scale quantum computing.
summary: This paper argues that monolithic quantum computing is physically impossible at large scales because classical control signals cannot keep up with quantum coherence. It proposes that we must move toward modular, heterogeneous architectures where different quantum platforms are linked via a network. By using a 'Reserve-Commit' protocol, the system can manage entanglement as a resource and convert timing failures into known erasure errors. The authors predict that this architectural shift becomes mandatory once a system reaches 100,000 to 1,000,000 physical qubits.
main_problem: The fundamental bottleneck in scaling quantum computing is a temporal mismatch where classical coordination latency grows with system size and eventually exceeds the bounded quantum coherence time.
main_result: The paper proves that modular, heterogeneous architectures are a physical necessity beyond a crossover scale of 10^5-10^6 qubits, as they replace superlinear geometric scaling penalties with manageable topological routing costs.
method: The authors use asymptotic scaling analysis and a formalization of a structural phase transition (1 + epsilon > gamma) to compare monolithic versus modular architectures, alongside a proposed 'Reserve-Commit' protocol for time-aware scheduling.
model_or_system: A large-scale, macroscopic quantum-classical hybrid system modeled via a Hamiltonian H = H_sys + H_ctrl + H_env, comparing monolithic arrays to modular architectures using Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC).
key_observables: Classical coordination latency (tau_c), quantum coherence time (tau_q), transduction efficiency (eta_trans), and the geometric penalty (epsilon).
important_parameters: Crossover scale (Nc) of 10^5-10^6 physical qubits, transduction target (eta_trans) ~ 0.1, and the coordination wall at approximately 8.3 x 10^4 qubits.
main_assumptions: Assumes no disruptive new technology emerges to resolve the isolation-interactability-topology trilemma and assumes the existence of a fixed hardware cost ratio (B/A).
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows a log-log plot of asymptotic structural cost; Figure 3 illustrates the conceptual shift from monolithic global unitaries to distributed LOCC; Figure 4 shows a timing diagram for the Reserve-Commit protocol; Figure 5 depicts an error degradation pipeline.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the scaling bottleneck, formalizes the geometric penalty and structural phase transition, proposes a modular/heterogeneous architecture and the Reserve-Commit protocol, and concludes with a quantitative prediction of the crossover scale and architectural convergence.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous physical argument for why the future of quantum computing must be distributed and heterogeneous, linking classical signal propagation limits to the fundamental limits of quantum error correction and architecture design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The Control Light Cone and geometric penalty in monolithic quantum architectures. A 3D spacetime representation where the X-Y plane denotes the physical qubit footprint and the Z-axis denotes time. The blue horizontal plane represents the coherence lifetime limit (τq). (a) At intermediate scales (N < Nc), the control signal cone (yellow) remains within the τq budget, enabling global coordination (τc ≪τq). (b) At utility scales (N > Nc), the spatial footprint expands such that the control cone breaches the τq horizon. The region exceeding the causal horizon (red shaded) incurs a superlinear geometric penalty (ϵ > 0), physically mandating modular decomposition to truncate this penalty.

FIG. 2. Scaling-induced architectural phase transition and modular crossover. Asymptotic structural cost C(N) versus physical qubit count N, plotted on a log-log scale, where the physical footprint relates to logical capacity as N ≈κNL, with κ ∼103 representing a typical surface-code overhead factor. The homogeneous architecture (red solid line) incurs a su- perlinear coordination penalty, Chom(N) = Θ(N 1+ϵ) with ϵ > 0, arising from finite signal propagation and control bandwidth constraints; the modular architecture (blue dashed line) achieves Cmod(N) = Θ(N γ), where γ reflects network routing eﬀi- ciency. Their intersection defines a crossover scale Nc(A, B, ηtrans) see Eq. (18)), beyond...

FIG. 3. Conceptual shift from monolithic integration to a distributed LOCC supply chain. Left: Monolithic global unitaries suffer from severe spatial crosstalk and global synchronization errors (τc > τq) as physical boundaries expand. Right: The distributed paradigm enforces strict modular isolation. Nonlocal operations are synthesized via Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC), consuming bipartite entanglement (managed as physical state ρij at the hardware layer, and abstracted as metadata tuples eij for classical scheduling) pre-distributed asynchronously by a dedicated quantum network, thus accommodating hardware heterogeneity while circumventing the geometric penalty.

FIG. 4. Time-aware Reserve–Commit Protocol timing diagram. The protocol execution flow is shown across three architectural layers (Classical Control Plane, Entanglement Management Layer, and Quantum Hardware Layer), constrained by the physical coherence deadline (tgen + τ (p) q , red line). In Scenario A (insuﬀicient temporal margin), the control plane performs a preemptive abort when tcurrent + τ ∗ exec > tgen + τ (p) q , explicitly outputting a semantic erasure marker (orange) to prevent scheduling-induced decoherence. In Scenario B (suﬀicient margin), the protocol commits to the execution of the LOCC sequence (Local Entangle →Measure →Coord →Feedforward) under the condition tcurrent + τ...

FIG. 5. The architectural semantic error degradation pipeline. The time-aware scheduling framework operates as a selective semantic classifier: heralded protocol failures (timeout aborts, heralded photon loss) are converted into location-known erasure metadata, while unheralded local decoherence bypasses the classifier as standard depolarizing noise. This structured routing supplies downstream erasure-aware QEC decoders with explicit error metadata, elevating the effective fault-tolerant threshold and reducing syndrome decoding complexity.
